dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a Sick Person call. A 55-year-old female reported chest pain.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call for help today?
I've had chest pain for two days, feels like pressure in my chest and arms.
You seem alert and oriented to person, place, time, and event. Are you aware of where you are?
Your GCS reads 15 (E4 V5 M6). You answered clearly and followed actions well.
Your eyes stayed open on their own without any prompting from us.
You expressed yourself clearly and remained oriented when we asked questions.
You followed through with movements asked, showing good understanding.
Could you confirm your name and date of birth for our records, please?
May we know a contact person for your file, and any regular medications or allergies?
No known drug allergies. I take Albuterol, aspirin, atorvastatin, among other medications regularly.
You have a history of primary hypertension, hypothyroidism, and non-insulin-dependent diabetes.
How would you describe the pain-sharp, dull, pressure? Does it move to any other areas?
It feels like tight pressure in the center of my chest extending into my arms. It doesn't spread further.
If 0 is no pain and 10 is the worst, where would you say your pain ranks now?
It was 7 but reduced to 5 after I took Labetolol earlier.
Your skin is warm and dry with normal color, and capillary refill is less than two seconds.
We don't note any deformities, crepitus, angulation, or other injuries on exam.
Your pupils are equal, round, reactive to light, and appear at 3 mm. No signs of JVD noted.
No fluid drainage, congestion, or other obstructions were found in your ears, nose, or mouth.
Breath sounds are clear and equal on both sides, and chest movement is normal.
Motor strength is positive in all extremities with full passive and active movement.
You remain alert and oriented to person, place, time, and event throughout our exam.
Your airway is patent, with free and unlabored breathing observed. No signs of obstruction.
Your pulse is 75, strong, and regular.
Respiratory rate is 16, even and unlabored.
Blood pressure is 167 over 101. We will track this closely over time.
Your glucose is 0. We'll note that, but it doesn't change our treatment.
SpO2 is 98% on room air, which is acceptable.
Your EKG reads in sinus rhythm with no acute life threats.
A 4-lead monitor is on for continuous cardiac observation.
A 12-lead EKG was obtained showing ST-T wave changes in inferior leads and first-degree AV block.
ST and T wave abnormality is noted in leads II and aVF.
Moderate evidence of left ventricular hypertrophy is noted on the 12-lead ECG.
Rechecked vitals: pulse 74, respirations 16, BP 186 over 120, SpO2 92%.
EKG remains in sinus rhythm with no significant added changes.
Radial pulses are strong and equal bilaterally.
We set up a 4-lead monitor and completed a 12-lead EKG as part of our assessment.
We are following the medical-chest pain-cardiac suspected protocol (2-1).
We're preparing to place you in a semi-Fowler's position with proper belts for safe transport.
[COMPANY_NAME] has been informed of your case. A yellow ED status has been assigned.
We will promptly transport you there. Please inform us if there are any symptom changes.
Your mental status remains at A&O x4, so no concern for altered consciousness.
Your breath sounds are clear in both lungs with no wheezing or crackles.
